attributes.stem: A 60-year-old man with known cirrhosis presents with a 12 month history of 8 kg weight loss, as well as generalised abdominal pain, without radiation. Stools and urine have been normal. His medical history includes significant alcohol and intravenous drug use. On examination, he appears jaundiced and his abdomen is distended. Shifting dullness is detected. Breath sounds are normal with no adventitious sounds and percussion note is resonant across both lung fields. A digital rectal examination reveals a smooth and slightly enlarged prostate. Bedside urine dipstick reveals protein 1+. Complete blood examination shows: Hb 99 g/dL [115-155], WCC 10.0 x 10*9/L [4.00-11] and Plt 110 x 10*9/L [150-450]. Biochemistry results are shown (Image).
attributes.stemQuestion: Which one of the following is the most likely diagnosis?
attributes.explanation:
Considering the presentation of weight loss, anorexia, and abdominal pain, occurring in a patient with known cirrhosis, the diagnosis of decompensation of his chronic liver disease is very likely. In this case, the history and examination findings are likely explained by new-onset ascites, a key feature in decompensated cirrhosis. Whilst patients can sometimes carry in excess of 10 litres of ascitic fluid, weight loss in end-stage cirrhosis is common, as patients are often malnourished and deplete of fat and protein stores. Liver function tests are usually abnormal in these patients, although they occasionally might be normal.

Patients presenting with congestive cardiac failure will often complain of shortness of breath and ankle swelling. Neither of these features seem to be present with this patient, and there seem to be no risk factors for cardiac failure, aside from heavy alcohol use (can cause dilated cardiomyopathy). On examination, there is no evidence to suggest pulmonary oedema or pleural effusion. Therefore this is an incorrect answer.

Nephrotic syndrome can sometimes cause ascites, however is an unlikely diagnosis in this patient, as there is no suggestion of peripheral oedema (e.g. ankles, hands, feet). Whilst his urine dipstick reveals proteinuria 1+, nephrotic syndrome is associated with a greater loss of protein in the urine (3+ or 4+). Furthermore, nephrotic syndrome does not explain the weight loss that this patient has experienced. Therefore this is an incorrect answer option.

Budd-Chiari syndrome should be considered in patients with a personal or family history of thrombophilia, who present with the classic triad of RUQ pain, hepatomegaly and ascites. There is no evidence to suggest a pro-coagulant state in this patient and his symptoms demonstrate a chronicity that is not consistent with a diagnosis of Budd-Chiari syndrome. Furthermore, a decompensation of chronic liver disease is much more common than Budd Chiari syndrome.

Carcinoma of the pancreatic head is made less likely by the absence of cholestatic symptoms (e.g, pale stools, dark urine). Furthermore, abdominal pain is a late finding and is more often seen with cancers of the pancreatic tail (along with radiation to the back).
learningPoints: Decompensated liver disease should always be considered in patients with known cirrhosis who have a recent deterioration in their health.
references: Raza A, Sood GK. Hepatocellular carcinoma review: Current treatment, and evidence-based medicine. World J Gastroenterol. 2014 Apr;20(15):4115-4127
